questions: At CyanGate, you led the development of the **JSON-to-JSON AI Agentic System**, which required integrating multiple LLM providers (OpenAI, Copilot, Gemini, Anthropic) and building a Python validation engine to cut costs and latency by 50%. During this project, you likely encountered moments where the path forward was unclear—perhaps a new LLM update broke existing workflows, or client requirements shifted mid-development. **Walk me through a specific instance where ambiguity threatened the project’s success. How did you navigate the uncertainty? What tradeoffs did you make, and how did you ensure alignment with both technical and business goals?**

Zephlen’s culture emphasizes *‘Radical Candor & Low Ego’*—we debate ideas vigorously but commit fully once a decision is made. In your **Atlassian Jira Intelligence System** project, you worked with stakeholders across teams (engineering, product, end-users) to replace manual ticket searches with an AI-powered assistant. **Describe a time when you had to challenge a stakeholder’s assumption or push back on a request that would have compromised the system’s reliability or scalability. How did you frame your argument? Did you face resistance, and if so, how did you handle it while maintaining the relationship?**

One of Zephlen’s core values is being *‘Agentic by Nature’*—taking ownership of problems even when they fall outside your role. Your CV mentions you managed **onboarding and technical mentoring for new AI engineering team members** at CyanGate. **Tell me about a time when you noticed a gap in the team’s processes, tools, or knowledge that wasn’t directly your responsibility to fix—perhaps in documentation, cross-team collaboration, or how requirements were gathered from clients. What did you do to address it? Did you face any pushback, and how did you measure the impact of your intervention?**
